---
title: "RAG Chatbot for Educational Purposes"
subtitle: "Technical Project Report"
date: "April 2026"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    theme: cosmo
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
---

## 1. Introduction

This report documents the design, development, and iterative improvement of an educational Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) chatbot built for the students of our class. The system allows students to ask natural language questions about their course notes and receive accurate, context-grounded answers powered by Anthropic's Claude language model.

The project was inspired by a practical opportunity within our class: one of our colleagues regularly publishes comprehensive course notes to a public GitHub repository, covering material across multiple subjects and semesters. Rather than manually searching through these notes, we wanted to take advantage of this shared resource by building a chatbot that fetches them directly from GitHub and makes them instantly queryable in natural language. Simply searching through the notes manually is time-consuming, and general-purpose AI assistants like ChatGPT have no knowledge of our specific course materials.

### 1.1 Project Goals

- Build a chatbot that answers questions exclusively based on provided course notes
- Ensure answers are grounded in retrieved context, not hallucinated
- Make the system easy to run locally without specialised infrastructure
- Keep note sources always up to date without manual file management
- Measure answer quality using established evaluation metrics

### 1.2 Technology Stack

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| LlamaIndex | Core RAG framework — chosen for its efficient indexing pipeline, retrieval and chat engine |
| Anthropic Claude | LLM (`claude-sonnet-4-6`) for answer generation |
| HuggingFace Embeddings | Local sentence embedding (`all-MiniLM-L6-v2`) |
| Streamlit | Web-based user interface |
| GitHub API | Live document source — notes fetched from repository |
| Python 3.13 | Runtime environment |

---

## 2. Methodology

We built the system iteratively, starting with a minimal working prototype and improving it step by step as we ran into practical challenges.

### 2.1 RAG Architecture

Retrieval-Augmented Generation grounds the LLM output in retrieved documents. The pipeline has four stages:

1. **Ingestion** — notes are split into 512-token chunks with 50-token overlap, keeping pieces small enough to be precise while the overlap ensures no context is lost at chunk boundaries
2. **Embedding** — each chunk is converted into a dense vector using a local sentence transformer, capturing its semantic meaning in a way that allows similarity-based search rather than simple keyword matching.
3. **Indexing** — vectors are stored in a `VectorStoreIndex`, persisted locally in `storage/`, so the index only needs to be built once and can be reloaded instantly on subsequent runs
4. **Retrieval and generation** — when a question is asked, the top 5 most semantically similar chunks are retrieved and passed to Claude as context, grounding the answer in the actual course notes rather than the model's general knowledge.

> **Key design choice:** The `condense_plus_context` chat mode rewrites the user question to be self-contained before retrieval, significantly improving quality in multi-turn conversations.

### 2.2 Phase 1 — Static Files

The first version used a static local `data/` folder. We manually downloaded the notes and placed them there before indexing. Notes were in `.qmd` format, requiring a manual rename to `.md` each time. This approach offered no way to detect updates, even if notes were updated weekly.

**Problems identified:**

- Notes had to be manually downloaded every time they were updated
- A shell command was required to rename `.qmd` files to `.md` before each run
- No mechanism existed to detect when notes had changed


### 2.3 Phase 2 — GitHub Crawling

The `data/` folder was replaced with `github_loader.py`, which fetches notes directly from the public repository of our colleague `nilsrechberger/mscids-notes` via the GitHub REST API. The crawler recursively traverses `hs25` and `fs26` folders, collects all `.qmd`, `.md`, and `.txt` files, and downloads them in parallel.

Each file becomes a LlamaIndex `Document` with structured metadata:

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| `semester` | Top-level folder (e.g. `hs25`, `fs26`) |
| `subject` | Second-level folder (e.g. `cip`, `statistics`) |
| `file_name` | The filename (e.g. `cip_notes.qmd`) |
| `file_path` | Full path within the repo |
| `source_url` | Direct GitHub URL to the file |
| `branch` | Git branch (default: `main`) |

### 2.4 Performance Optimisations

| Optimisation | Effect |
|---|---|
| Index caching | Persisted to `storage/` — subsequent starts load in under 2 seconds |
| Faster embedding model | Switched to `all-MiniLM-L6-v2` — lighter and faster than `bge-small` |
| Parallel file fetching | `ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=10)` — approx. 5x faster than sequential |
| GitHub token | Added Personal Access Token — resolved 403 rate limit (60 to 5,000 req/hr) |

---

## 3. Main Results

### 3.1 RAGAS Evaluation — Summary

Answer quality was measured using a RAGAS-inspired evaluation suite. Fourteen reference questions covering the main course topics were each paired with a manually written ground truth answer. Claude acts as LLM judge, scoring four metrics per question.

| Metric | Score |
|---|---|
| Faithfulness | **98.25%** |
| Answer Relevancy | **92.43%** |
| Context Precision | **93.89%** |
| Context Recall | **96.43%** |
| **Overall (harmonic mean)** | **95.20%** |

> **Interpretation:** The chatbot achieved an overall RAGAS score of 95.2%. Faithfulness is the highest metric at 98.2%, confirming the system rarely introduces claims not supported by the retrieved course notes — critical for an educational tool.

### 3.2 Per-Question Breakdown

| Question | Faithfulness | Ans. Rel. | Ctx. Prec. | Ctx. Rec. |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Observational vs experimental research design? | 94.1% | 100% | 100% | 100% |
| Four main dimensions of data quality? | 100% | 85% | 100% | 100% |
| Classical vs Bayesian statistics? | 92.9% | 72% | 100% | 100% |
| What is a p-value and what does a low one mean? | 92.3% | 95% | 80.6% | **50%** |
| Purpose and types of blinding in experiments? | 100% | 100% | 100% | 100% |
| What is web scraping? | 100% | 100% | 100% | 100% |
| Purpose of virtual environments in Python? | 100% | 90% | 100% | 100% |
| What is R and what is it used for? | 100% | 100% | 100% | 100% |
| When to apply log-transformations? | 100% | 85% | 100% | 100% |
| What is a confounder in research design? | 100% | 95% | 91.7% | 100% |
| What is R-squared in regression? | 100% | 82% | **75%** | 100% |
| Generalised Linear Models — what and when? | 96.2% | 95% | 91.7% | 100% |
| Logit link function for binomial data? | 100% | 95% | 100% | 100% |
| Assumptions of linear model about errors? | 100% | 100% | 75.6% | 100% |

Two questions scored lower on specific metrics: the p-value question had context recall of 50%, and the R-squared question had context precision of 75%. Both suggest the relevant note sections may be sparse or fragmented across multiple chunks.

---

## 4. Outlook

While the chatbot already works well for personal local use, we see several promising directions to develop it into a more complete solution for our class.

### 4.1 Incremental Re-indexing

Refresh currently rebuilds the entire index from scratch. Checking GitHub API file timestamps and only re-embedding changed files would reduce refresh time from minutes to seconds.

### 4.2 Multi-user Deployment

Deploying the chatbot as a shared service for our class would require a centralised vector database (Chroma or Qdrant) and a hosting solution such as Streamlit Community Cloud or a cloud VM, allowing all students to benefit from it without running it locally.

### 4.4 Source Citation in Answers

Since each document carries semester, subject, and filename metadata, instructing the model to append a source reference to each answer would allow students to trace answers directly back to our colleague's notes.

### 4.5 Automated Re-indexing on Push

A GitHub Actions workflow could trigger re-indexing automatically when new commits are pushed to the notes repository, keeping the chatbot always in sync.

> **Priority next step:** Incremental re-indexing (4.1) and multi-user deployment (4.2) go hand in hand — making the system fast enough to refresh before every session and accessible to the whole class without requiring a local setup.

---

## 5. Conclusion

This project delivered a working RAG chatbot tailored to our class, built on open and accessible tools. Starting from a simple prototype with static files, the system evolved through five well-defined phases into a robust application that automatically fetches our colleague's latest course notes from GitHub, indexes them efficiently, and answers our questions using Claude.

The RAGAS evaluation confirms the system performs at a high level. With an overall score of 95.2% and a faithfulness score of 98.2%, the chatbot reliably grounds its answers in the actual notes published by our colleague — ensuring responses are always tied to our specific course material.

| Milestone | Phase |
|---|---|
| Static `data/` folder with manual `.md` files | Phase 1 |
| GitHub crawler with `.qmd` support (sequential) | Phase 2 |
| Parallel fetching + faster embedding + cache strategy | Phase 3 |
| GitHub token + sidebar Refresh button | Phase 4 |
| RAGAS evaluation — overall score 95.2% | Phase 5 |

---

## Appendix — File Overview

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `app.py` | Streamlit web interface — main entry point |
| `main.py` | Command-line interface — alternative entry point |
| `github_loader.py` | GitHub API crawler — parallel fetch, rich metadata |
| `evaluate.py` | RAGAS evaluation — 14 questions, 4 metrics |
| `test_env.py` | Verifies API keys are loaded from `.env` |
| `requirements.txt` | Python dependencies |
| `.env` | API keys — never committed to version control |
| `storage/` | Auto-generated index cache — delete to force re-index |
| `.gitignore` | Excludes `.env`, `storage/`, `__pycache__`, `.venv` |

### Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` | Anthropic Claude API key |
| `LLAMA_CLOUD_API_KEY` | LlamaCloud API key |
| `GITHUB_TOKEN` | GitHub Personal Access Token (`public_repo` scope) |
| `GITHUB_OWNER` | GitHub username of the notes repository owner |
| `GITHUB_REPO` | Repository name (e.g. `mscids-notes`) |
| `GITHUB_BRANCH` | Branch to crawl (default: `main`) |
| `GITHUB_FOLDERS` | Comma-separated folders to crawl (e.g. `hs25,fs26`) |
